# Transcrição: 05-Using the Small Business Administrationâs Loans

## [00:00 - 03:00]

In this next lesson, we're looking at small business administration loans, or SBA loans. These are government backed loans, but these funds don't come directly from the small business administration. They actually come from a commercial bank like JP Morgan, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, and so on. Could be your local bank for that matter. And you can use the funds to buy an operating business, replace debt, or purchase things like machinery, equipment, and land. The downside to an SBA loan is that they have stricter requirements compared to other business loans. These include things like minimum credit score, no bankruptcies in the past three years, minimum down payment, but there's also a lot of attractive things about an SBA loan if you meet those requirements. First, we'll start by looking at some of the main requirements, and then we can dive deep into the benefits and numbers. Keep in mind that this is an overview at a particular point in time, but the rules are always changing. So be sure to check for the latest criteria programs and news from the SBA's website on your screen. Let's talk about some of the SBA loan requirements next. Obviously, to be eligible for a small business administration loan, you have to be a small business, and the SBA uses three things to measure what qualifies as a small business. One, the number of employees. Having more than a certain number means you no longer qualify as a small business. The threshold depends on the type of industry, too. For one industry, it might be considered small if it has 20 employees. Another industry might be considered small if you have 50 employees. So you need to understand for your particular needs and requirements in industry what the rules and regulations are specifically for you. The same goes for the second item on the list, which is revenue. When your revenue exceeds a certain threshold, which depends on the industry, you're no longer considered a small business. And the third is the only one that doesn't differ among industries, and that's net worth. To be eligible for an SBA loan, your net worth has to be under $15 million, and your annual income has to be less than $5 million. Next, the business you're acquiring has to be within an eligible industry. Basically, the SBA forbids certain industries from receiving these type of loans. The business has to be for profit. The business can't be engaged in loan packaging, speculation, gambling, lending, or investments. The SBA.gov website has a clear list of ineligible businesses on its website, so you'll want to verify the business that you're looking at does not meet those requirements or you won't be eligible for a loan. Another eligibility requirement is that all guarantors have to be citizens or permanent residents of the U.S. Guarantors are the main applicants who are going to be held responsible for paying off the loan in full.

## [03:00 - 06:04]

From the SBA's perspective, non-residents are too difficult to try to track down in the event of a default. The SBA will want a guarantor who has experience in a related field. They want to know that the business is in good hands. They want to be confident that the business owner has the high likelihood of succeeding in order to pay back the loan. They'll look into your personal background, your citizenship status, your address, your criminal records, as well as personal credit reports and income taxes. They'll want to see your credit rating. The minimum credit score they look for is 640, but you really want to be above 680. If you're below the 680 mark, you want to have a good credit history. They'll also ask for a lot of business-related information, whether it's applicable depending on your experience, current circumstances, the business you're trying to buy. This will be things like business bank account statements, financial and income statements, balance sheet, debt schedules. They'll want to see a resume detailing company management experience, a business plan, all your legal documents, your articles of incorporation, your license, your leases, all the documents that you have are going to be required as part of an SBA loan. They'll want to see everything. If a guarantor approaches them and has no assets, no experience, they're not going to approve the loan. On the flip side, even though the SBA loans have more stringent requirements, they have attractive interest rates, closing costs, and repayment terms. So how do these SBA loans work? Let's start diving deep into the nitty gritty. The maximum size of an SBA loan is $5 million and the maximum length of the loan is 10 years. The repayment cycle would be monthly. The SBA guarantees 75% of the loan amount and the lender is responsible for 15%. So effectively 90% of your loan is guaranteed. The lender has real money at stake here in these deals. So they'll treat the deal as though it's a commercial transaction. They'll go through their typical credit process. Then there are the terms and fees. The first one to consider is the guarantee fee, which is 3% of the guaranteed amount in the deal. Next, there are certain fees which will range from 0.25% to 3.5%, depending on the gross size of the loan. There are loan packaging fees, which will end up being several thousand dollars as well. The interest rate will usually be somewhere around 7% to 10%. This depends on the size of the loan and which lender you ultimately use. And if you have a seller note in your deal, there will be accountability safeguards in place as well. If your deal includes a seller note, at least 5% of the purchase price to the company has to remain unpaid. SBA lenders will also take a collateral charge on all the assets in the business. So it becomes easier to get a loan if you have more assets. The SBA loan lender isn't necessarily going to ensure that there are enough secured assets to cover the whole loan amount. Unfortunately you'd like to see that, but it's not a deal breaker.

## [06:04 - 07:10]

Let's look at the process of applying for an SBA loan. There are hundreds of SBA lenders. You want to find and work with a preferred lender. A preferred lender will already have a relationship with the SBA. And because that lender already is trusted, the SBA won't necessarily need to review all the paperwork that's been submitted. It will speed up the process. If you're not with a preferred lender, everything goes to the SBA and that can slow things down. You know how our government can be. A good working relationship with an SBA preferred lender is also beneficial because not only are they effective, easier to work with, and can speed up the timeline, they can also help with the paperwork because there's quite a bit of SBA loan paperwork. That's SBA loans in a nutshell. They can be an incredible tool if you need them. But obviously, you need to have all your ducks lined up when filing the application. Like I said, there are many options to help you finance your deal. I'll be covering even more in the upcoming lessons. I'll talk to you soon and I'll see you there.
